2026-04-20.title: Recovery: Easy Ride (Level 2, Level 3)
2026-04-20.description:
1H @ 45-55% FTP.

PURPOSE: Allow for adaptations from previously challenging workouts, whilst maintaining rhythm of training without adding any further stress to the body. Also helps to circulate oxygenated blood around the body and remove any metabolic waste products that may be lingering in the muscles from these previous workouts, since lactate is combusted as a fuel in the aerobic energy process better at a lower power output than it is when completely resting.

2026-04-20.title: VO2Max: 30S/15S Microburst Interval Blocks (Level 1)
2026-04-20.description:
20M building up from 55-70% FTP, including a 45S effort at main interval set intensity after ~10M to warm up.

3x 9M blocks, each comprising: 16x (30S @ 120-130% FTP/15S @ 30-40% FTP) with 4-5M easy spinning as active recovery between each block of micro intervals.

Remaining time @ ~50-65% FTP.

See workout details below for full session breakdown.

PURPOSE: Increase the maximal volume of oxygen the body can take in and deliver to the working muscles. Stimulates improvements in stroke volume and mitochondrial function. This particular workout design also improves speed of recovery between hard efforts (lactate buffering), enhances the ability to overcome force due to the need to re-accelerate multiple times, and will also serve to increase anaerobic capacity and VLamax, given that a large amount of lactate can accumulate during the course of each block of micro-intervals.

The seminal study investigating the benefits of this style of microburst intervals and to observe a beneficial effect on aerobic and anaerobic capacity is this study by Tabata et al.:
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/8897392/

More recent research by Rønnestad et al. suggests that microburst intervals might out-perform the classic-style VO2max intervals in developing aerobic capacity:
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24382021/
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31977120/

2026-04-20.title: Test: Fatigue Resistance Test (Level 1, Level 2, Level 3)
2026-04-20.description:
Ride for ~20M to warm up (feel free to extend this a little further if you need longer to get to a suitable test location).

Then complete a 1M effort at the maximum power you think you can sustain across the effort.

Then ride for around 2-3H (depending on existing endurance abilities), keeping power between 55-70% FTP as much as possible.

Then complete another 60S effort at a maximal effort.

Finally, complete remainder of ride at a Zone 2 (55-70% FTP) intensity.

PURPOSE: This session also allows you to assess your fatigue resistance. You can compare your 1M power at the start and end of the ride of the ride, to see how much this has fallen (we recommend working of the % decline). Use this test to assess improvements in your fatigue resistance.

2026-04-27.title: Muscular Endurance: Medium Low Cadence Efforts (Level 1)
2026-04-27.description:
Start with ~20M warm up in the range of 45-65% FTP.

Then complete 4x 7.5M @ 85-92% FTP and 2.5M @ 50-60% FTP. Use a low cadence for muscular endurance intervals of ~60-75RPM to increase force demands on muscle fibres. This session could be done as a set of hill repeats.

After this long set of tempo intervals, cool down for remainder of ride @ 50-60% FTP.

PURPOSE: To develop greater fatigue resistance or muscular endurance through sustained work that places higher force demand on the muscle fibres. In doing so, you train the muscle fibres to become more resistance to micro tears and general damage. When muscle fibres (especially slow twitch or Type I fibres) become damaged because of a relatively high sustained workloads, they have to call upon the far less aerobically-efficient Type II fibres to help maintain a consistent power output an carry the load. This has the effect of reducing what's called your 'exercise economy/efficiency', which essentially means you need to supply more oxygen (and fuel) for the same power output (very similar to the fuel efficiency of a car). This is one of the reasons you can see heart rate drift for a given power as you become fatigued in a race or long training session, since the heart is required to pump more oxygen to sustain the workload once the muscles become fatigued. By improving your exercise economy via greater resistance to fatigue/damage, you're able to improve your endurance at a broad range of intensities.

These low-cadence tempo intervals also specifically target aerobic adaptations within Type IIa muscle fibres, which typically aren't substantially activated in a lower intensity endurance ride until the latter stages of the ride. These intervals are good for reducing VLamax, and thus improving lactate threshold.

2026-04-13.title: Endurance: Steady Ride (Level 2, Level 3)
2026-04-13.description:
3H @ 60-75% Max HR.

Don't worry about straying out of the target intensity range for short periods on steeper climbs or descents - just try for as much time within target zone as possible. Try to choose as flat or rolling course if possible to maximise time in target zone.

PURPOSE: Enhances aerobic fitness by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarization in the muscles, development of slow-twitch muscles fibres and trains body to more efficiently use fat as primary fuel source.

Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance.

You can read more about the importance of low-intensity training such as this in this great article by Stephen Seiler and Espen Tønnessen:
https://sportsci.org/2009/ss.htm

2026-04-27.title: Muscular Endurance: Shorter Low Cadence Efforts
2026-04-27.description:
Start with ~20M warm up in the range of 45-65% FTP.

Then complete 6x 4M @ 85-92% FTP and 2.5M @ 50-60% FTP. Use a low cadence for muscular endurance intervals of ~60-65RPM to increase force demands on muscle fibres. This session could be done as a set of hill repeats.

After this long set of tempo intervals, cool down for remainder of ride @ 50-60% FTP.

PURPOSE: To develop greater fatigue resistance or muscular endurance through sustained work that places higher force demand on the muscle fibres. In doing so, you train the muscle fibres to become more resistance to micro tears and general damage. When muscle fibres (especially slow twitch or Type I fibres) become damaged because of a relatively high sustained workloads, they have to call upon the far less aerobically-efficient Type II fibres to help maintain a consistent power output an carry the load. This has the effect of reducing what's called your 'exercise economy/efficiency', which essentially means you need to supply more oxygen (and fuel) for the same power output (very similar to the fuel efficiency of a car). This is one of the reasons you can see heart rate drift for a given power as you become fatigued in a race or long training session, since the heart is required to pump more oxygen to sustain the workload once the muscles become fatigued. By improving your exercise economy via greater resistance to fatigue/damage, you're able to improve your endurance at a broad range of intensities.

These low-cadence tempo intervals also specifically target aerobic adaptations within Type IIa muscle fibres, which typically aren't substantially activated in a lower intensity endurance ride until the latter stages of the ride. These intervals are good for reducing VLamax, and thus improving lactate threshold.

2026-04-27.title: Muscular Endurance: Breakthrough Session (Level 2)
2026-04-27.description:
NOTE: this is a challenging session, and should only be used sparingly (e.g. once every 4-6 weeks). It should usually be reserved for the 2-3 months leading into your key competition.

20M warm-up, building up to 60-75% Max HR.

Then ride for majority of ride at a tempo intensity, aiming to keep HR between 70-85% Max HR as much as possible.

Cool down in final 20-mins, bringing HR down to 60-75% Max HR.

PURPOSE: This is a 'breakthrough' session, which aims to provide a higher training stress than normal, and simulate some conditions of a long endurance race. Breakthrough sessions like these really help to develop your endurance in particular, and also help with mental and logistical preparedness for racing (e.g. allowing opportunity to test out your race nutrition).

2026-04-20.title: Endurance: Long Steady Ride (Level 3)
2026-04-20.description:
4H @ 60-70% FTP.

Keep intensity in check wherever possible, but don't stress straying into Zone 1 on downhills or Zone 3 on steeper climbs.

PURPOSE: Enhances aerobic endurance by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarisation around the slow twitch (Type I) muscle fibres and trains body to more efficiently use fat as primary fuel source, reducing the use of carbohydrates for fuel and resulting in a greater lactate threshold since less lactate is produced of a byproduct of glycolysis (breakdown of carbohydrates).

Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance.

You can read more about the importance of low-intensity training such as this in this great article by Stephen Seiler and Espen Tønnessen:
https://sportsci.org/2009/ss.htm

2026-04-13.title: Endurance: Steady Ride (Level 2, Level 3)
2026-04-13.description:
3H @ 60-70% FTP.

Keep intensity in check wherever possible, but don't stress straying into Zone 1 on downhills or Zone 3 on steeper climbs.

PURPOSE: Enhances aerobic fitness by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarization in the muscles, development of slow-twitch muscles fibres and trains body to more efficiently use fat as primary fuel source.

Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance.

You can read more about the importance of low-intensity training such as this in this great article by Stephen Seiler and Espen Tønnessen:
https://sportsci.org/2009/ss.htm

2026-04-20.title: Endurance: Steady Ride with Mid-Ride Sprints (Level 2, Level 3)
2026-04-20.description:
2H @ 65-75% Max HR. Towards the middle of the ride, include 4x10S max sprints with 2M50S easy spinning between each sprint.

Remaining time after sprints @ 60-75% Max HR.

PURPOSE: Enhances aerobic fitness by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarization in the muscles, development of slow-twitch muscles fibres and trains body to more efficiently use fat as primary fuel source. Also builds fatigue resistance and neuromuscular power alongside basic endurance.

Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance.

You can read more about the importance of low-intensity training such as this in this great article by Stephen Seiler and Espen Tønnessen:
https://sportsci.org/2009/ss.htm

You can read more about the benefits of sprint training here: https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/sms.14133

2026-04-20.title: Recovery: Easy Ride + Tempo Increases (Level 2, Level 3)
2026-04-20.description:
45M @ 45-50% FTP with 1M @ 70-75% FTP (7/10 effort) every 9M throughout ride.

PURPOSE: Allow for adaptations from previously challenging workouts, whilst maintaining rhythm of training without adding any further stress to the body. Also helps to circulate oxygenated blood around the body and remove any metabolic waste products that may be lingering in the muscles from these previous workouts, since lactate is combusted as a fuel in the aerobic energy process better at a lower power output than it is when completely resting. This ride adds a little bit of intensity for when it's important for the legs not to get "sleepy".

PURPOSE: Enhances aerobic fitness by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarization in the muscles, development of slow-twitch muscles fibres and trains body to more efficiently use fat as primary fuel source.

Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance.

2026-04-27.title: Wk 6 - Race Specific stuff
2026-04-27.description:
Competition Phase - Race Specific sessions !
----------------------------------------------------
We will incorporate 3 hard days, 4 easy days or similar !
----------------------------------------------------
Now we need to specific for the event coming up. So training in race conditions such as
Heat, hill efforts, road surface. Make sure we discuss gear, tyres etc.
----------------------------------------------------
This block will include max efforts in sessions 30-60s !!
Increased intensity but decreased volume.
----------------------------------------------------
Running economy is now also key, add drills, stretches and form sessions.
----------------------------------------------------
Race efforts in SBR.
Efforts close to the race demands.
----------------------------------------------------

2026-04-20.title: Anaerobic Power: 60S Repeats (Level 2)
2026-04-20.description:
20M warm up, building up from 55-65% FTP towards 85-95% FTP, and then settling in to 55-75% FTP. Add in 2-3 10S surges at ~8/10 effort level to fully activate your muscles before the anaerobic efforts.

Then perform 5x 1M intervals @ a near maximal effort, paced consistently across the 1M interval (for most people this will be about 130-150% FTP, but for others it may be higher/lower). The first time you do this workout you may need to do this based on feel, but in subsequent workouts you will have a better idea of what power you can target. The goal is to hit a power that you can hold consistently across each of the intervals, so don't go so hard on the first one that you have nothing left for the last ones.

Take 6M for active recovery @ 40-50% FTP (i.e. very easy spinning) after each interval. Take longer to recover if you feel you need to - the focus of this workout is to achieve maximal power in each interval, so the recovery can be as long as necessary to achieve this.

Remaining time up to ~1-1H30M @ 50-65% FTP to return body to resting levels steadily.

See workout details below for further details.

PURPOSE: The main effect of these intervals is to increase peak anaerobic power – that is the maximal rate at which energy can be produced anaerobically. This includes factors such as increased activity of enzymes associated with both the phosphocreatine system and the glycolytic system, and also increased glycogen storage within the muscles (Bangsbo et al., 2006).

In addition, these intervals can also contribute to improved anaerobic stamina through improved buffering of hydrogen ions (Bangsbo et al., 2006), and potential improvements in lactate transport (Pilegaard et al., 1999), aerobic capacity (Burgomaster et al., 2006) and mitochondrial function (Bishop et al., 2014, Tripp et al., 2022).

Bangsbo, J., Mohr, M., Poulsen, A., Perez-Gomez, J., & Krustrup, P. (2006). Training and testing the elite athlete. J Exerc Sci Fit, 4(1), 1-14.

Bishop, D. J., Granata, C., & Eynon, N. (2014). Can we optimise the exercise training prescription to maximise improvements in mitochondria function and content?. Biochimica et Biophysica Acta (BBA)-General Subjects, 1840(4), 1266-1275.

Burgomaster, K. A., Heigenhauser, G. J., & Gibala, M. J. (2006). Effect of short-term sprint interval training on human skeletal muscle carbohydrate metabolism during exercise and time-trial performance. Journal of applied physiology, 100(6), 2041-2047.

Pilegaard, H., Domino, K., Noland, T., Juel, C., Hellsten, Y., Halestrap, A. P., & Bangsbo, J. (1999). Effect of high-intensity exercise training on lactate/H+ transport capacity in human skeletal muscle. American Journal of Physiology-Endocrinology And Metabolism, 276(2), E255-E261.

Tripp, T. R., Frankish, B. P., Lun, V., Wiley, J. P., Shearer, J., Murphy, R. M., & MacInnis, M. J. (2022). Time course and fibre type‐dependent nature of calcium‐handling protein responses to sprint interval exercise in human skeletal muscle. The Journal of Physiology.

2026-04-20.title: Muscular Endurance: Shorter Low Cadence Efforts (Level 1)
2026-04-20.description:
Start with ~20M warm up in the range of 45-65% FTP.

Then complete 6x 5M @ 85-92% FTP and 2.5M @ 50-60% FTP. Use a low cadence for muscular endurance intervals of ~60-75RPM to increase force demands on muscle fibres. This session could be done as a set of hill repeats.

After this long set of tempo intervals, cool down for remainder of ride @ 50-60% FTP.

PURPOSE: To develop greater fatigue resistance or muscular endurance through sustained work that places higher force demand on the muscle fibres. In doing so, you train the muscle fibres to become more resistance to micro tears and general damage. When muscle fibres (especially slow twitch or Type I fibres) become damaged because of a relatively high sustained workloads, they have to call upon the far less aerobically-efficient Type II fibres to help maintain a consistent power output an carry the load. This has the effect of reducing what's called your 'exercise economy/efficiency', which essentially means you need to supply more oxygen (and fuel) for the same power output (very similar to the fuel efficiency of a car). This is one of the reasons you can see heart rate drift for a given power as you become fatigued in a race or long training session, since the heart is required to pump more oxygen to sustain the workload once the muscles become fatigued. By improving your exercise economy via greater resistance to fatigue/damage, you're able to improve your endurance at a broad range of intensities.

These low-cadence tempo intervals also specifically target aerobic adaptations within Type IIa muscle fibres, which typically aren't substantially activated in a lower intensity endurance ride until the latter stages of the ride. These intervals are good for reducing VLamax, and thus improving lactate threshold.

2026-04-27.description:

PURPOSE: Enhances aerobic fitness by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarisation around the muscles, fatigue resistance of slow-twitch muscles fibres and trains body to more efficiently use fat as primary substrate.

The Zone 3 blocks also help develop aerobic abilities within and around higher-power muscle fibres, and may improve fatigue-resistance by developing muscular strength.

Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance.

2026-04-20.title: Swim| 12 x(3 x 50 PB&PD) + 3 x 200 PB&PD | Strength
Threshold: 4x 4min (Level 1) | FTP
2026-04-20.description:
20-30min warm up building up to 50-70% FTP.

4x 4min @ 103-108% FTP with 3min active recovery between each interval. Best done on shallow grade climb if riding outdoors, standing on the pedals for most of the intervals.

Remaining time @ ~50-60% FTP - can cool down longer to 90min if time allows.

PURPOSE: Improve muscular endurance and lactate threshold, as well as improve body's ability to deliver large amounts of oxygen to working muscles.

These intervals work on the principle of the 'VO2 slow component' (see e.g. here: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21552162/). At intensities between the lactate threshold and the maximal aerobic power, oxygen intake gradually rises towards VO2max.

These intervals are also inspired by the following study, which shows supra-threshold intervals to be more tolerable than classic VO2max intervals: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28051345/

While we’re not aware of any scientific research supporting this, we also believe these supra-threshold intervals likely contribute to improved lactate shuttling and buffering. Theoretically, this makes sense as lactate levels are held at a high level throughout the intervals, placing stress on the lactate shuttling and buffering systems.

2026-04-27.description:
1H15M @ 45-50% FTP with 1M @ 70-75% FTP (7/10 effort) every 9M throughout ride.

PURPOSE: Allow for adaptations from previously challenging workouts, whilst maintaining rhythm of training without adding any further stress to the body. Also helps to circulate oxygenated blood around the body and remove any metabolic waste products that may be lingering in the muscles from these previous workouts, since lactate is combusted as a fuel in the aerobic energy process better at a lower power output than it is when completely resting. This ride adds a little bit of intensity for when it's important for the legs not to get "sleepy".

PURPOSE: Enhances aerobic fitness by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarization in the muscles, development of slow-twitch muscles fibres and trains body to more efficiently use fat as primary fuel source.

Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance.

You can read more about the importance of low-intensity training such as this in this great article by Stephen Seiler and Espen Tønnessen:

2026-04-20.title: Muscular Endurance: Shorter Low Cadence Efforts (Level 1)
2026-04-20.description:
Start with ~20M warm up in the range of 45-65% FTP.

Then complete 6x 5M @ 85-92% FTP and 2.5M @ 50-60% FTP. Use a low cadence for muscular endurance intervals of ~60-75RPM to increase force demands on muscle fibres. This session could be done as a set of hill repeats.

After this long set of tempo intervals, cool down for remainder of ride @ 50-60% FTP.

PURPOSE: To develop greater fatigue resistance or muscular endurance through sustained work that places higher force demand on the muscle fibres. In doing so, you train the muscle fibres to become more resistance to micro tears and general damage. When muscle fibres (especially slow twitch or Type I fibres) become damaged because of a relatively high sustained workloads, they have to call upon the far less aerobically-efficient Type II fibres to help maintain a consistent power output an carry the load. This has the effect of reducing what's called your 'exercise economy/efficiency', which essentially means you need to supply more oxygen (and fuel) for the same power output (very similar to the fuel efficiency of a car). This is one of the reasons you can see heart rate drift for a given power as you become fatigued in a race or long training session, since the heart is required to pump more oxygen to sustain the workload once the muscles become fatigued. By improving your exercise economy via greater resistance to fatigue/damage, you're able to improve your endurance at a broad range of intensities.

These low-cadence tempo intervals also specifically target aerobic adaptations within Type IIa muscle fibres, which typically aren't substantially activated in a lower intensity endurance ride until the latter stages of the ride. These intervals are good for reducing VLamax, and thus improving lactate threshold.

PURPOSE: To develop greater fatigue resistance or muscular endurance through sustained work that places higher force demand on the muscle fibres. In doing so, you train the muscle fibres to become more resistance to micro tears and general damage. When muscle fibres (especially slow twitch or Type I fibres) become damaged because of a relatively high sustained workloads, they have to call upon the far less aerobically-efficient Type II fibres to help maintain a consistent power output an carry the load. This has the effect of reducing what's called your 'exercise economy/efficiency', which essentially means you need to supply more oxygen (and fuel) for the same power output (very similar to the fuel efficiency of a car). This is one of the reasons you can see heart rate drift for a given power as you become fatigued in a race or long training session, since the heart is required to pump more oxygen to sustain the workload once the muscles become fatigued. By improving your exercise economy via greater resistance to fatigue/damage, you're able to improve your endurance at a broad range of intensities.

These low-cadence tempo intervals also specifically target aerobic adaptations within Type IIa muscle fibres, which typically aren't substantially activated in a lower intensity endurance ride until the latter stages of the ride. These intervals are good for reducing VLamax, and thus improving lactate threshold.

2026-05-04.title: Anaerobic Power: 30S Repeats (Level 2)
2026-05-04.description:
30M warm up, building up from 55-65% FTP towards 85-95% FTP, and then settling in to 55-75% FTP. Add in 2-3 10S surges at ~8/10 effort level to fully activate your muscles before the anaerobic efforts.

Then perform 8x 30S intervals @ a near maximal effort, paced consistently across the 30S interval (for most people this will be about 130-150% FTP, but for others it may be higher/lower). The first time you do this workout you may need to do these efforts based on feel, but in subsequent workouts you will have a better idea of what power you can target. The goal is to hit a power that you can hold consistently across each of the intervals, so don't go so hard on the first one that you have nothing left for the last ones.

Take 3M for active recovery @ 30-40% FTP (i.e. very easy spinning) after each interval. Take longer to recover if you feel you need to - the focus of this workout is to achieve maximal power in each interval, so the recovery can be as long as necessary to achieve this.

Remaining time up to ~1-1H30M @ 50-65% FTP to return body to resting levels steadily.

See workout details below for further details.

PURPOSE: The main effect of these intervals is to increase peak anaerobic power – that is the maximal rate at which energy can be produced anaerobically. This includes factors such as increased activity of enzymes associated with both the phosphocreatine system and the glycolytic system, and also increased glycogen storage within the muscles (Bangsbo et al., 2006).

In addition, these intervals can also contribute to improved anaerobic stamina through improved buffering of hydrogen ions (Bangsbo et al., 2006), and potential improvements in lactate transport (Pilegaard et al., 1999), aerobic capacity (Burgomaster et al., 2006) and mitochondrial function (Bishop et al., 2014, Tripp et al., 2022).

Bangsbo, J., Mohr, M., Poulsen, A., Perez-Gomez, J., & Krustrup, P. (2006). Training and testing the elite athlete. J Exerc Sci Fit, 4(1), 1-14.

Bishop, D. J., Granata, C., & Eynon, N. (2014). Can we optimise the exercise training prescription to maximise improvements in mitochondria function and content?. Biochimica et Biophysica Acta (BBA)-General Subjects, 1840(4), 1266-1275.

Burgomaster, K. A., Heigenhauser, G. J., & Gibala, M. J. (2006). Effect of short-term sprint interval training on human skeletal muscle carbohydrate metabolism during exercise and time-trial performance. Journal of applied physiology, 100(6), 2041-2047.

Pilegaard, H., Domino, K., Noland, T., Juel, C., Hellsten, Y., Halestrap, A. P., & Bangsbo, J. (1999). Effect of high-intensity exercise training on lactate/H+ transport capacity in human skeletal muscle. American Journal of Physiology-Endocrinology And Metabolism, 276(2), E255-E261.

Tripp, T. R., Frankish, B. P., Lun, V., Wiley, J. P., Shearer, J., Murphy, R. M., & MacInnis, M. J. (2022). Time course and fibre type‐dependent nature of calcium‐handling protein responses to sprint interval exercise in human skeletal muscle. The Journal of Physiology.

2026-05-04.title: Muscular Endurance: 2x 20M (Level 2)
2026-05-04.description:
20M building up from 50-70% FTP, including a short 2M effort @ 90% FTP to warm up for the intensity of the main set of intervals. Try to also keep cadence low during this short effort to further mimic the demands of the intervals.

2x 20M @ 85-92% of FTP. Try to incorporate some riding @ a lower cadence of 60-75RPM, unless this results in knee issues arising, in which case raise cadence slightly. 5M easy spinning in between two intervals for active recovery.

Remaining time gradually warming down from 70-45% FTP.

PURPOSE: To develop greater fatigue resistance or muscular endurance through sustained work that places higher force demand on the muscle fibres. In doing so, you train the muscle fibres to become more resistance to micro tears and general damage. When muscle fibres (especially slow twitch or Type I fibres) become damaged because of a relatively high sustained workloads, they have to call upon the far less aerobically-efficient Type II fibres to help maintain a consistent power output an carry the load. This has the effect of reducing what's called your 'exercise economy/efficiency', which essentially means you need to supply more oxygen (and fuel) for the same power output (very similar to the fuel efficiency of a car). This is one of the reasons you can see heart rate drift for a given power as you become fatigued in a race or long training session, since the heart is required to pump more oxygen to sustain the workload once the muscles become fatigued. By improving your exercise economy via greater resistance to fatigue/damage, you're able to improve your endurance at a broad range of intensities.

These low-cadence tempo intervals also specifically target aerobic adaptations within Type IIa muscle fibres, which typically aren't substantially activated in a lower intensity endurance ride until the latter stages of the ride. These intervals are good for reducing VLamax, and thus improving lactate threshold.

2026-04-20.title: Endurance: 90min Fasted Morning Ride (HR)
2026-04-20.description:
90min @ 60-75% Max HR in fasted state.

Fasted state can be achieved by training in the morning, before eating any breakfast or other carbohydrate source. Take some food/energy drink on the ride with you in case you need some fuel before the ride is over. But try to ride as long as possible without eating.

Don't worry about straying out of the target intensity range for short periods on steeper climbs or descents - just try for as much time within target zone as possible. Try to choose as flat or rolling course if possible to maximise time in target zone.

PURPOSE: Enhances aerobic fitness by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarization in the muscles, development of slow-twitch muscles fibres and trains body to more efficiently use fat as primary fuel source.

Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance.

You can read more about the importance of low-intensity training such as this in this great article by Stephen Seiler and Espen Tønnessen:
https://sportsci.org/2009/ss.htm

2026-04-20.title: Indoor Endurance: High Variation Aerobic Ride (Level 1)
2026-04-20.description:
1H05M alternating between 1M @ 60% FTP, 3M @ 70% FTP and 1M @ 75% FTP throughout.

Warm up gradually from 50-65% FTP and cool down to 45% by the end of the workout.

Feel free to change up cadence throughout ride from a lower level of ~75-85RPM and upper level of 95-105RPM.

If doing this ride outdoors, don't worry about alternating the power. Just keep this between 60-75% FTP.

PURPOSE: Enhances aerobic fitness by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarization in the muscles, development of slow-twitch muscles fibres and trains body to more efficiently use fat as primary fuel source.

Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance.

You can read more about the importance of low-intensity training such as this in this great article by Stephen Seiler and Espen Tønnessen:
https://sportsci.org/2009/ss.htm

2026-04-27.title: Recovery: Easy Ride + Short Ramps (Level 3)
2026-04-27.description:
20M building up to 50-60% FTP.

2x 10M ramps from 70% FTP up to 90% FTP with 15M easy @ 50-60% FTP in between.

See Workout Details below for exact breakdown.

Remaining time up to 1H15M @ 50-60% FTP.

PURPOSE: Allow for adaptations from previously challenging workouts, whilst maintaining rhythm of training without adding any further stress to the body. Also helps to circulate oxygenated blood around the body and remove any metabolic waste products that may be lingering in the muscles from these previous workouts, since lactate is combusted as a fuel in the aerobic energy process better at a lower power output than it is when completely resting. This ride adds a little bit of intensity for when it's important for the legs not to get "sleepy".

2026-04-20.title: Endurance: Steady Ride + Anaerobic Mainteinance (Level 2, Level 3)
2026-04-20.description:
Ride 30M @ 60-75% Max HR, keeping intensity in check on any hills.

Then include 3x 40S intervals @ 10/10 effort with 3M20S easy spinning between.

Remainder of ride @ 60-75% Max HR.

PURPOSE: The initial sprints are designed to help maintain your anaerobic abilities without compromising the main goal of this session, which is to enhance aerobic fitness by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarization in the muscles, development of slow-twitch muscles fibres and strengthening the heart muscle. This session also includes some anaerobic efforts to help maintain anaerobic capacity.

Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance.

You can read more about the importance of low-intensity training such as this in this great article by Stephen Seiler and Espen Tønnessen:
https://sportsci.org/2009/ss.htm

2026-04-27.description:
3H10M @ 60-70% Max HR including 10M tempo blocks @ ~75-85% Max HR every 30M throughout ride.

PURPOSE: Enhances aerobic fitness by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarization in the muscles, development of slow-twitch muscles fibres and trains body to more efficiently use fat as primary fuel source. In addition, increases muscle's ability to resist fatigue (muscular endurance).

The Zone 3 blocks also help develop aerobic abilities within and around higher-power muscle fibres, and may improve fatigue-resistance by developing muscular strength.

Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance.

2026-05-04.title: Threshold: 4x 4min (Level 1) | FTP
2026-05-04.description:
20-30min warm up building up to 50-70% FTP.

4x 4min @ 103-108% FTP with 3min active recovery between each interval. Best done on shallow grade climb if riding outdoors, standing on the pedals for most of the intervals.

Remaining time @ ~50-60% FTP - can cool down longer to 90min if time allows.

PURPOSE: Improve muscular endurance and lactate threshold, as well as improve body's ability to deliver large amounts of oxygen to working muscles.

These intervals work on the principle of the 'VO2 slow component' (see e.g. here: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21552162/). At intensities between the lactate threshold and the maximal aerobic power, oxygen intake gradually rises towards VO2max.

These intervals are also inspired by the following study, which shows supra-threshold intervals to be more tolerable than classic VO2max intervals: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28051345/

While we’re not aware of any scientific research supporting this, we also believe these supra-threshold intervals likely contribute to improved lactate shuttling and buffering. Theoretically, this makes sense as lactate levels are held at a high level throughout the intervals, placing stress on the lactate shuttling and buffering systems.

2026-04-20.title: Muscular Endurance: Shorter Low Cadence Efforts (Level 1)
2026-04-20.description:
Start with ~20M warm up in the range of 45-65% FTP.

Then complete 6x 5M @ 85-92% FTP and 2.5M @ 50-60% FTP. Use a low cadence for muscular endurance intervals of ~60-75RPM to increase force demands on muscle fibres. This session could be done as a set of hill repeats.

After this long set of tempo intervals, cool down for remainder of ride @ 50-60% FTP.

PURPOSE: To develop greater fatigue resistance or muscular endurance through sustained work that places higher force demand on the muscle fibres. In doing so, you train the muscle fibres to become more resistance to micro tears and general damage. When muscle fibres (especially slow twitch or Type I fibres) become damaged because of a relatively high sustained workloads, they have to call upon the far less aerobically-efficient Type II fibres to help maintain a consistent power output an carry the load. This has the effect of reducing what's called your 'exercise economy/efficiency', which essentially means you need to supply more oxygen (and fuel) for the same power output (very similar to the fuel efficiency of a car). This is one of the reasons you can see heart rate drift for a given power as you become fatigued in a race or long training session, since the heart is required to pump more oxygen to sustain the workload once the muscles become fatigued. By improving your exercise economy via greater resistance to fatigue/damage, you're able to improve your endurance at a broad range of intensities.

These low-cadence tempo intervals also specifically target aerobic adaptations within Type IIa muscle fibres, which typically aren't substantially activated in a lower intensity endurance ride until the latter stages of the ride. These intervals are good for reducing VLamax, and thus improving lactate threshold.

2026-04-20.title: Tempo Run | 12/10/8min MMM (3) | Pace

2026-04-27.title: VO2Max: 30S/15S Microburst Interval Blocks (Level 1)
2026-04-27.description:
20M building up from 55-70% FTP, including a 45S effort at main interval set intensity after ~10M to warm up.

3x 9M blocks, each comprising: 16x (30S @ 120-130% FTP/15S @ 30-40% FTP) with 4-5M easy spinning as active recovery between each block of micro intervals.

Remaining time @ ~50-65% FTP.

See workout details below for full session breakdown.

PURPOSE: Increase the maximal volume of oxygen the body can take in and deliver to the working muscles. Stimulates improvements in stroke volume and mitochondrial function. This particular workout design also improves speed of recovery between hard efforts (lactate buffering), enhances the ability to overcome force due to the need to re-accelerate multiple times, and will also serve to increase anaerobic capacity and VLamax, given that a large amount of lactate can accumulate during the course of each block of micro-intervals.

The seminal study investigating the benefits of this style of microburst intervals and to observe a beneficial effect on aerobic and anaerobic capacity is this study by Tabata et al.:
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/8897392/

More recent research by Rønnestad et al. suggests that microburst intervals might out-perform the classic-style VO2max intervals in developing aerobic capacity:
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24382021/
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31977120/

2026-04-27.title: Hills | 6-8% 1x 15/30/45s for 20mins | Pace

2026-04-27.title: Muscular Endurance: Medium Low Cadence Efforts (Level 1)
2026-04-27.description:
Start with ~20M warm up in the range of 45-65% FTP.

Then complete 4x 7.5M @ 85-92% FTP and 2.5M @ 50-60% FTP. Use a low cadence for muscular endurance intervals of ~60-75RPM to increase force demands on muscle fibres. This session could be done as a set of hill repeats.

After this long set of tempo intervals, cool down for remainder of ride @ 50-60% FTP.

PURPOSE: To develop greater fatigue resistance or muscular endurance through sustained work that places higher force demand on the muscle fibres. In doing so, you train the muscle fibres to become more resistance to micro tears and general damage. When muscle fibres (especially slow twitch or Type I fibres) become damaged because of a relatively high sustained workloads, they have to call upon the far less aerobically-efficient Type II fibres to help maintain a consistent power output an carry the load. This has the effect of reducing what's called your 'exercise economy/efficiency', which essentially means you need to supply more oxygen (and fuel) for the same power output (very similar to the fuel efficiency of a car). This is one of the reasons you can see heart rate drift for a given power as you become fatigued in a race or long training session, since the heart is required to pump more oxygen to sustain the workload once the muscles become fatigued. By improving your exercise economy via greater resistance to fatigue/damage, you're able to improve your endurance at a broad range of intensities.

These low-cadence tempo intervals also specifically target aerobic adaptations within Type IIa muscle fibres, which typically aren't substantially activated in a lower intensity endurance ride until the latter stages of the ride. These intervals are good for reducing VLamax, and thus improving lactate threshold.

2026-05-04.title: Test: Fatigue Resistance Test (Level 1, Level 2, Level 3)
2026-05-04.description:
Ride for ~20M to warm up (feel free to extend this a little further if you need longer to get to a suitable test location).

Then complete a 1M effort at the maximum power you think you can sustain across the effort.

Then ride for around 2-3H (depending on existing endurance abilities), keeping power between 55-70% FTP as much as possible.

Then complete another 60S effort at a maximal effort.

Finally, complete remainder of ride at a Zone 2 (55-70% FTP) intensity.

PURPOSE: This session also allows you to assess your fatigue resistance. You can compare your 1M power at the start and end of the ride of the ride, to see how much this has fallen (we recommend working of the % decline). Use this test to assess improvements in your fatigue resistance.

2026-04-20.title: Endurance: 2H Fasted Morning Ride (Level 2, Level 3)
2026-04-20.description:
2H @ 60-75% Max HR in fasted state.

Fasted state can be achieved by training in the morning, before eating any breakfast or other carbohydrate source. Take some food/energy drink on the ride with you in case you need some fuel before the ride is over. But try to ride as long as possible without eating.

Don't worry about straying out of the target intensity range for short periods on steeper climbs or descents - just try for as much time within target zone as possible. Try to choose as flat or rolling course if possible to maximise time in target zone.

PURPOSE: Enhances aerobic fitness by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarization in the muscles, development of slow-twitch muscles fibres and trains body to more efficiently use fat as primary fuel source.

Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance.

You can read more about the importance of low-intensity training such as this in this great article by Stephen Seiler and Espen Tønnessen:
https://sportsci.org/2009/ss.htm

2026-04-13.title: Preparation: Pre-Race Openers (Level 1, Level 2, Level 3)
2026-04-13.description:
10-15M warm up @ 55-70% FTP.

3x 1M @ 95-105% FTP, each short effort followed by ~2M @ 50-60% FTP.

10-15M steady @ 55-60% FTP (can be a little higher) then a further 2x efforts of 1M @ 95-105% FTP followed by 30S at a typical VO2max intensity of 110-125% FTP. Take 2M or so easy spinning between efforts.

Finish the ride up to ~1H with a cool down of more steady riding of 55-65% FTP.

PURPOSE: Prepare the body for tomorrow's competition by maintaining rhythm of training, circulating oxygenated blood and lactate, and recruiting the Type IIa and IIx fibres briefly without causing/adding fatigue that could hinder performance in upcoming race/event. This will help prevent the legs from becoming lethargic and to maintain usual metabolism ahead of competition.

2026-04-20.title: 15x 1min ON/OFF (55 rpm) @ 90%|FTP
2026-04-20.description:
Please follow the workout details, with prescribed zones (and power targets if required) outlined below.
-
The repeats should be performed in a moderate to steep climb. Select a high gear which is hard to turn. The primary objective is to produce high torque. Aim to produce the highest amount of torque you are able to sustain for the 10 minutes and across all intervals. Simplified, push as hard on the pedals as you can at the low cadence.

Expect you heart rate to rise slower than normal due to the lower cadence, it should rise slowly through zone 3 and may even go into zone 4 or higher. Stay relaxed during climbs.
Stand for 50 metres if you cannot sustain seated position. This session is about keeping your core stable and generating a round pedalling action. Focus on pulling up and pushing over the top of the pedal stroke.

2026-04-20.title: Muscular Endurance: Shorter Low Cadence Efforts
2026-04-20.description:
Start with ~20M warm up in the range of 45-65% FTP.

Then complete 6x 4M @ 85-92% FTP and 2.5M @ 50-60% FTP. Use a low cadence for muscular endurance intervals of ~60-65RPM to increase force demands on muscle fibres. This session could be done as a set of hill repeats.

After this long set of tempo intervals, cool down for remainder of ride @ 50-60% FTP.

PURPOSE: To develop greater fatigue resistance or muscular endurance through sustained work that places higher force demand on the muscle fibres. In doing so, you train the muscle fibres to become more resistance to micro tears and general damage. When muscle fibres (especially slow twitch or Type I fibres) become damaged because of a relatively high sustained workloads, they have to call upon the far less aerobically-efficient Type II fibres to help maintain a consistent power output an carry the load. This has the effect of reducing what's called your 'exercise economy/efficiency', which essentially means you need to supply more oxygen (and fuel) for the same power output (very similar to the fuel efficiency of a car). This is one of the reasons you can see heart rate drift for a given power as you become fatigued in a race or long training session, since the heart is required to pump more oxygen to sustain the workload once the muscles become fatigued. By improving your exercise economy via greater resistance to fatigue/damage, you're able to improve your endurance at a broad range of intensities.

These low-cadence tempo intervals also specifically target aerobic adaptations within Type IIa muscle fibres, which typically aren't substantially activated in a lower intensity endurance ride until the latter stages of the ride. These intervals are good for reducing VLamax, and thus improving lactate threshold.

2026-04-13.title: Endurance: Mid-Length Steady Ride (Level 1, Level 2, Level 3)
2026-04-13.description:
2H @ 60-70% FTP.

Keep intensity in check wherever possible, but don't stress straying into Zone 1 on downhills or Zone 3 on steeper climbs.

PURPOSE: Enhances aerobic fitness by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarization in the muscles, development of slow-twitch muscles fibres and trains body to more efficiently use fat as primary fuel source.

Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance.

You can read more about the importance of low-intensity training such as this in this great article by Stephen Seiler and Espen Tønnessen:
https://sportsci.org/2009/ss.htm

2026-04-20.title: Anaerobic Power: 60S Repeats (Level 2)
2026-04-20.description:
30M warm up, building up from 55-65% FTP towards 85-95% FTP, and then settling in to 55-75% FTP. Add in 2-3 10S surges at ~8/10 effort level to fully activate your muscles before the anaerobic efforts.

Then perform 5x 1M intervals @ a near maximal effort, paced consistently across the 1M interval (for most people this will be about 130-150% FTP, but for others it may be higher/lower). The first time you do this workout you may need to do this based on feel, but in subsequent workouts you will have a better idea of what power you can target. The goal is to hit a power that you can hold consistently across each of the intervals, so don't go so hard on the first one that you have nothing left for the last ones.

Take 6M for active recovery @ 40-50% FTP (i.e. very easy spinning) after each interval. Take longer to recover if you feel you need to - the focus of this workout is to achieve maximal power in each interval, so the recovery can be as long as necessary to achieve this.

Remaining time up to ~1-1H30M @ 50-65% FTP to return body to resting levels steadily.

See workout details below for further details.

PURPOSE: The main effect of these intervals is to increase peak anaerobic power – that is the maximal rate at which energy can be produced anaerobically. This includes factors such as increased activity of enzymes associated with both the phosphocreatine system and the glycolytic system, and also increased glycogen storage within the muscles (Bangsbo et al., 2006).

In addition, these intervals can also contribute to improved anaerobic stamina through improved buffering of hydrogen ions (Bangsbo et al., 2006), and potential improvements in lactate transport (Pilegaard et al., 1999), aerobic capacity (Burgomaster et al., 2006) and mitochondrial function (Bishop et al., 2014, Tripp et al., 2022).

Bangsbo, J., Mohr, M., Poulsen, A., Perez-Gomez, J., & Krustrup, P. (2006). Training and testing the elite athlete. J Exerc Sci Fit, 4(1), 1-14.

Bishop, D. J., Granata, C., & Eynon, N. (2014). Can we optimise the exercise training prescription to maximise improvements in mitochondria function and content?. Biochimica et Biophysica Acta (BBA)-General Subjects, 1840(4), 1266-1275.

Burgomaster, K. A., Heigenhauser, G. J., & Gibala, M. J. (2006). Effect of short-term sprint interval training on human skeletal muscle carbohydrate metabolism during exercise and time-trial performance. Journal of applied physiology, 100(6), 2041-2047.

Pilegaard, H., Domino, K., Noland, T., Juel, C., Hellsten, Y., Halestrap, A. P., & Bangsbo, J. (1999). Effect of high-intensity exercise training on lactate/H+ transport capacity in human skeletal muscle. American Journal of Physiology-Endocrinology And Metabolism, 276(2), E255-E261.

Tripp, T. R., Frankish, B. P., Lun, V., Wiley, J. P., Shearer, J., Murphy, R. M., & MacInnis, M. J. (2022). Time course and fibre type‐dependent nature of calcium‐handling protein responses to sprint interval exercise in human skeletal muscle. The Journal of Physiology.

2026-04-13.title: Endurance: Steady Ride + Fatigue Resistance Intervals (Level 3)
2026-04-13.description:
2H45M @ 60-70% FTP, then 3-4x 2.5M intervals @ 98-103% FTP with 1.5M easy spinning between each interval for active recovery.

Finally cool down for remainder of ride @ 55-65% FTP.

PURPOSE: Enhances aerobic fitness by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarization in the muscles, development of slow-twitch muscles fibres and trains body to more efficiently use fat as primary fuel source. This session has the additional benefit of teaching your body to work hard in the presence of fatigue. These aerobic changes happen as a function of time rather than intensity, so achieving the target duration is most important.

Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance.

The late-stage efforts also help develop aerobic abilities within higher-power muscle fibres, and improve your ability to ride at higher wattages in the presence of existing fatigue.

You can read more about the importance of low-intensity training such as this in this great article by Stephen Seiler and Espen Tønnessen:
https://sportsci.org/2009/ss.htm

2026-04-27.title: Endurance: Steady Ride + Muscular Endurance Blocks (Level 3)
2026-04-27.description:
3H10M @ 60-65% FTP including 10M muscular endurance blocks @ ~85-90% FTP every 30M throughout ride. Perform these blocks at a lower cadence (60-70RPM) to help emphasise the demand on the muscles for power production and lessen the involvement of the cardiovascular system.

PURPOSE: Enhances aerobic fitness by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarisation around the muscles, fatigue resistance of slow-twitch muscles fibres and trains body to more efficiently use fat as primary substrate.

The Zone 3 blocks also help develop aerobic abilities within and around higher-power muscle fibres, and may improve fatigue-resistance by developing muscular strength.

Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance.

2026-05-04.title: Supra-Threshold: 4x5M (Level 1)
2026-05-04.description:
20-30M warm up building up to 50-70% FTP.

4x 5M @ 103-108% FTP with 3M active recovery between each interval. Best done on shallow grade climb if riding outdoors, standing on the pedals for most of the intervals.

Remaining time @ ~50-60% FTP.

PURPOSE: Improve muscular endurance and lactate threshold, as well as improve body's ability to deliver large amounts of oxygen to working muscles.

These intervals work on the principle of the 'VO2 slow component' (see e.g. here: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21552162/). At intensities between the lactate threshold and the maximal aerobic power, oxygen intake gradually rises towards VO2max.

These intervals are also inspired by the following study, which shows supra-threshold intervals to be more tolerable than classic VO2max intervals: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28051345/

While we’re not aware of any scientific research supporting this, we also believe these supra-threshold intervals likely contribute to improved lactate shuttling and buffering. Theoretically, this makes sense as lactate levels are held at a high level throughout the intervals, placing stress on the lactate shuttling and buffering systems.

2026-04-20.title: Anaerobic Power: 60S Repeats (Level 3)
2026-04-20.description:
30M warm up, building up from 55-65% FTP towards 85-95% FTP, and then settling in to 55-75% FTP. Add in 2-3 10S surges at ~8/10 effort level to fully activate your muscles before the anaerobic efforts.

Then perform 5-6x 1M intervals @ a near maximal effort, paced consistently across the 1M interval (for most people this will be about 130-150% FTP, but for others it may be higher/lower). The first time you do this workout you may need to do this based on feel, but in subsequent workouts you will have a better idea of what power you can target. The goal is to hit a power that you can hold consistently across each of the intervals, so don't go so hard on the first one that you have nothing left for the last ones.

Take 6M for active recovery @ 40-50% FTP (i.e. very easy spinning) after each interval. Take longer to recover if you feel you need to - the focus of this workout is to achieve maximal power in each interval, so the recovery can be as long as necessary to achieve this.

Remaining time up to 1H30M @ 50-65% FTP to return body to resting levels steadily.

See workout details below for further details.

PURPOSE: The main effect of these intervals is to increase peak anaerobic power – that is the maximal rate at which energy can be produced anaerobically. This includes factors such as increased activity of enzymes associated with both the phosphocreatine system and the glycolytic system, and also increased glycogen storage within the muscles (Bangsbo et al., 2006).

In addition, these intervals can also contribute to improved anaerobic stamina through improved buffering of hydrogen ions (Bangsbo et al., 2006), and potential improvements in lactate transport (Pilegaard et al., 1999), aerobic capacity (Burgomaster et al., 2006) and mitochondrial function (Bishop et al., 2014, Tripp et al., 2022).

Bangsbo, J., Mohr, M., Poulsen, A., Perez-Gomez, J., & Krustrup, P. (2006). Training and testing the elite athlete. J Exerc Sci Fit, 4(1), 1-14.

Bishop, D. J., Granata, C., & Eynon, N. (2014). Can we optimise the exercise training prescription to maximise improvements in mitochondria function and content?. Biochimica et Biophysica Acta (BBA)-General Subjects, 1840(4), 1266-1275.

Burgomaster, K. A., Heigenhauser, G. J., & Gibala, M. J. (2006). Effect of short-term sprint interval training on human skeletal muscle carbohydrate metabolism during exercise and time-trial performance. Journal of applied physiology, 100(6), 2041-2047.

Pilegaard, H., Domino, K., Noland, T., Juel, C., Hellsten, Y., Halestrap, A. P., & Bangsbo, J. (1999). Effect of high-intensity exercise training on lactate/H+ transport capacity in human skeletal muscle. American Journal of Physiology-Endocrinology And Metabolism, 276(2), E255-E261.

Tripp, T. R., Frankish, B. P., Lun, V., Wiley, J. P., Shearer, J., Murphy, R. M., & MacInnis, M. J. (2022). Time course and fibre type‐dependent nature of calcium‐handling protein responses to sprint interval exercise in human skeletal muscle. The Journal of Physiology.

2026-04-20.title: Endurance: 90min Fasted Morning Ride (HR)
2026-04-20.description:
90min @ 60-75% Max HR in fasted state.

Fasted state can be achieved by training in the morning, before eating any breakfast or other carbohydrate source. Take some food/energy drink on the ride with you in case you need some fuel before the ride is over. But try to ride as long as possible without eating.

Don't worry about straying out of the target intensity range for short periods on steeper climbs or descents - just try for as much time within target zone as possible. Try to choose as flat or rolling course if possible to maximise time in target zone.

PURPOSE: Enhances aerobic fitness by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarization in the muscles, development of slow-twitch muscles fibres and trains body to more efficiently use fat as primary fuel source.

Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance.

You can read more about the importance of low-intensity training such as this in this great article by Stephen Seiler and Espen Tønnessen:
https://sportsci.org/2009/ss.htm

2026-04-27.title: 15x 1min ON/OFF (55 rpm) @ 90%|FTP
2026-04-27.description:
Please follow the workout details, with prescribed zones (and power targets if required) outlined below.
-
The repeats should be performed in a moderate to steep climb. Select a high gear which is hard to turn. The primary objective is to produce high torque. Aim to produce the highest amount of torque you are able to sustain for the 10 minutes and across all intervals. Simplified, push as hard on the pedals as you can at the low cadence.

Expect you heart rate to rise slower than normal due to the lower cadence, it should rise slowly through zone 3 and may even go into zone 4 or higher. Stay relaxed during climbs.
Stand for 50 metres if you cannot sustain seated position. This session is about keeping your core stable and generating a round pedalling action. Focus on pulling up and pushing over the top of the pedal stroke.

2026-04-13.title: Preparation: Pre-Race Openers (Level 1, Level 2, Level 3)
2026-04-13.description:
10-15M warm up @ 55-70% FTP.

3x 1M @ 95-105% FTP, each short effort followed by ~2M @ 50-60% FTP.

10-15M steady @ 55-60% FTP (can be a little higher) then a further 2x efforts of 1M @ 95-105% FTP followed by 30S at a typical VO2max intensity of 110-125% FTP. Take 2M or so easy spinning between efforts.

Finish the ride up to ~1H with a cool down of more steady riding of 55-65% FTP.

PURPOSE: Prepare the body for tomorrow's competition by maintaining rhythm of training, circulating oxygenated blood and lactate, and recruiting the Type IIa and IIx fibres briefly without causing/adding fatigue that could hinder performance in upcoming race/event. This will help prevent the legs from becoming lethargic and to maintain usual metabolism ahead of competition.

2026-04-20.title: Muscular Endurance: Breakthrough Session (Level 3)
2026-04-20.description:
NOTE: this is a challenging session, and should only be used sparingly (e.g. once every 4-6 weeks). It should usually be reserved for the 2-3 months leading into your key competition.

20M warm-up, building up to 60-75% Max HR.

Then ride for majority of ride at a tempo intensity, aiming to keep HR between 70-85% Max HR as much as possible.

Cool down in final 20-mins, bringing HR down to 60-75% Max HR.

PURPOSE: This is a 'breakthrough' session, which aims to provide a higher training stress than normal, and simulate some conditions of a long endurance race. Breakthrough sessions like these really help to develop your endurance in particular, and also help with mental and logistical preparedness for racing (e.g. allowing opportunity to test out your race nutrition).

2026-04-27.title: Wk 6 - Race Specific stuff
2026-04-27.description:
Competition Phase - Race Specific sessions !
----------------------------------------------------
We will incorporate 3 hard days, 4 easy days or similar !
----------------------------------------------------
Now we need to specific for the event coming up. So training in race conditions such as

PURPOSE: Enhances aerobic fitness by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarization in the muscles, development of slow-twitch muscles fibres and trains body to more efficiently use fat as primary fuel source.

Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance.

You can read more about the importance of low-intensity training such as this in this great article by Stephen Seiler and Espen Tønnessen:

2026-05-04.title: Muscular Endurance: Medium Low-Cadence Efforts (Level 1.2)
2026-05-04.description:
20M warm-up, building up to 60-75% Max HR. Optionally include a 1-2M effort at around a 7/10 effort level to fully activate your legs. This usually makes the main set of intervals feel easier.

4x 10M @ 75-85% Max HR, riding at a relatively low cadence (70-80 rpm). Take 2.5M easy riding @ 50-60% Max HR (at your usual self-selected cadence) between each block. This session could be done as a set of hill repeats.

Remainder of ride @ 60-75% Max HR.

Feel free to alter cadence throughout workout, as well as pedalling style (seated and standing).

PURPOSE: To develop greater fatigue resistance or muscular endurance through sustained work that places higher force demand on the muscle fibres. In doing so, you train the muscle fibres to become more resistance to micro tears and general damage. When muscle fibres (especially slow twitch or Type I fibres) become damaged because of a relatively high sustained workloads, they have to call upon the far less aerobically-efficient Type II fibres to help maintain a consistent power output an carry the load. This has the effect of reducing what's called your 'exercise economy/efficiency', which essentially means you need to supply more oxygen (and fuel) for the same power output (very similar to the fuel efficiency of a car). This is one of the reasons you can see heart rate drift for a given power as you become fatigued in a race or long training session, since the heart is required to pump more oxygen to sustain the workload once the muscles become fatigued. By improving your exercise economy via greater resistance to fatigue/damage, you're able to improve your endurance at a broad range of intensities.

These low-cadence tempo intervals also specifically target aerobic adaptations within Type IIa muscle fibres, which typically aren't substantially activated in a lower intensity endurance ride until the latter stages of the ride. These intervals are good for reducing VLamax, and thus improving lactate threshold.

PURPOSE: Enhances aerobic endurance by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarisation around the slow twitch (Type I) muscle fibres and trains body to more efficiently use fat as primary fuel source, reducing the use of carbohydrates for fuel and resulting in a greater lactate threshold since less lactate is produced of a byproduct of glycolysis (breakdown of carbohydrates).

Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance.

You can read more about the importance of low-intensity training such as this in this great article by Stephen Seiler and Espen Tønnessen:

2026-04-20.title: Indoor Endurance: Undulating Aerobic Ride (Level 1, Level 2, Level 3)
2026-04-20.description:
2H alternating between roughly 5M @ 55-65% FTP and 5M 65-75% FTP. Warm up initially by building up from 50-70% FTP and cool down with 10M reducing from around 70% to 45% FTP.

Feel free to change up cadence throughout ride from a lower level of ~75-85RPM and upper level of 95-105RPM.

If doing this ride outdoors, don't worry about alternating the power. Just try to keep between 55-75% FTP.

PURPOSE: This workout serves to target and develop the aerobic energy system and encourages adaptations like greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the sites within the muscle cells where aerobic metabolism takes place), enhanced capillarization in the muscles (more blood vessels for the blood to both travel to and from the muscles through), and optimises substrate (fuel source) utilisation to allow the body to combust greater amounts of lipids (fat) at greater power outputs in preference to glycogen, which the body only has limited stores of.

Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance.

You can read more about the importance of low-intensity training such as this in this great article by Stephen Seiler and Espen Tønnessen:
https://sportsci.org/2009/ss.htm

2026-04-20.title: Recovery: Easy Ride + Tempo Increases (Level 2, Level 3)
2026-04-20.description:
45M @ 45-50% FTP with 1M @ 70-75% FTP (7/10 effort) every 9M throughout ride.

PURPOSE: Allow for adaptations from previously challenging workouts, whilst maintaining rhythm of training without adding any further stress to the body. Also helps to circulate oxygenated blood around the body and remove any metabolic waste products that may be lingering in the muscles from these previous workouts, since lactate is combusted as a fuel in the aerobic energy process better at a lower power output than it is when completely resting. This ride adds a little bit of intensity for when it's important for the legs not to get "sleepy".

2026-04-20.title: Threshold: 4x 4min (Level 1)
2026-04-20.description:
20-30min warm up building up to 50-70% FTP.

4x 4min @ 103-108% FTP with 3min active recovery between each interval. Best done on shallow grade climb if riding outdoors, standing on the pedals for most of the intervals.

Remaining time @ ~50-60% FTP - can cool down longer to 90min if time allows.

PURPOSE: Improve muscular endurance and lactate threshold, as well as improve body's ability to deliver large amounts of oxygen to working muscles.

These intervals work on the principle of the 'VO2 slow component' (see e.g. here: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21552162/). At intensities between the lactate threshold and the maximal aerobic power, oxygen intake gradually rises towards VO2max.

These intervals are also inspired by the following study, which shows supra-threshold intervals to be more tolerable than classic VO2max intervals: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28051345/

While we’re not aware of any scientific research supporting this, we also believe these supra-threshold intervals likely contribute to improved lactate shuttling and buffering. Theoretically, this makes sense as lactate levels are held at a high level throughout the intervals, placing stress on the lactate shuttling and buffering systems.

Warm up gradually from 50-65% FTP and cool down to 45% by the end of the workout.

Feel free to change up cadence throughout ride from a lower level of ~75-85RPM and upper level of 95-105RPM.

If doing this ride outdoors, don't worry about alternating the power. Just keep this between 60-75% FTP.

PURPOSE: Enhances aerobic fitness by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarization in the muscles, development of slow-twitch muscles fibres and trains body to more efficiently use fat as primary fuel source.

Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance.

You can read more about the importance of low-intensity training such as this in this great article by Stephen Seiler and Espen Tønnessen:
https://sportsci.org/2009/ss.htm

2026-04-27.title: Endurance: Steady Ride + Anaerobic Maintenance (Level 2)
2026-04-27.description:
30M @ 60-70% FTP.

Then 3x 40S intervals paced at the maximal power you can produce consistently across the 40S effort (the power target shown is just for illustrative purposes). Take at least 3M20S to recover after each interval.

Remainder of ride @ 55-75% FTP.

PURPOSE: The inclusion of Early-stage sprints is intended to help maintain your anaerobic abilities, without comprising the key goal of this session, which is to enhance aerobic fitness by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarization in the muscles, development of slow-twitch muscles fibres and strengthening the heart muscle. This session also includes some anaerobic efforts to help maintain anaerobic capacity.

Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance.

You can read more about the importance of low-intensity training such as this in this great article by Stephen Seiler and Espen Tønnessen:

2026-04-13.title: Endurance: Steady Ride + Muscular Endurance Blocks (Level 3)
2026-04-13.description:
3H10M @ 60-65% FTP including 10M muscular endurance blocks @ ~85-90% FTP every 30M throughout ride. Perform these blocks at a lower cadence (60-70RPM) to help emphasise the demand on the muscles for power production and lessen the involvement of the cardiovascular system.

PURPOSE: Enhances aerobic fitness by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarisation around the muscles, fatigue resistance of slow-twitch muscles fibres and trains body to more efficiently use fat as primary substrate.

The Zone 3 blocks also help develop aerobic abilities within and around higher-power muscle fibres, and may improve fatigue-resistance by developing muscular strength.

Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance.

2026-04-20.title: Endurance: Steady Ride + Fatigue Resistance Intervals (Level 3)
2026-04-20.description:
2H45M @ 60-70% FTP, then 3-4x 2.5M intervals @ 98-103% FTP with 1.5M easy spinning between each interval for active recovery.

Finally cool down for remainder of ride @ 55-65% FTP.

PURPOSE: Enhances aerobic fitness by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarization in the muscles, development of slow-twitch muscles fibres and trains body to more efficiently use fat as primary fuel source. This session has the additional benefit of teaching your body to work hard in the presence of fatigue. These aerobic changes happen as a function of time rather than intensity, so achieving the target duration is most important.

Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance.

The late-stage efforts also help develop aerobic abilities within higher-power muscle fibres, and improve your ability to ride at higher wattages in the presence of existing fatigue.

You can read more about the importance of low-intensity training such as this in this great article by Stephen Seiler and Espen Tønnessen:
https://sportsci.org/2009/ss.htm

2026-04-27.description:

Also helps to circulate oxygenated blood around the body and remove any metabolic waste products that may be lingering in the muscles from these previous workouts, since lactate is combusted as a fuel in the aerobic energy process better at a lower power output than it is when completely resting. This ride adds a little bit of intensity for when it's important for the legs not to get "sleepy".

2026-04-27.title: Muscular Endurance: Shorter Low Cadence Efforts
2026-04-27.description:
Start with ~20M warm up in the range of 45-65% FTP.

Then complete 6x 4M @ 85-92% FTP and 2.5M @ 50-60% FTP. Use a low cadence for muscular endurance intervals of ~60-65RPM to increase force demands on muscle fibres. This session could be done as a set of hill repeats.

After this long set of tempo intervals, cool down for remainder of ride @ 50-60% FTP.

PURPOSE: To develop greater fatigue resistance or muscular endurance through sustained work that places higher force demand on the muscle fibres. In doing so, you train the muscle fibres to become more resistance to micro tears and general damage. When muscle fibres (especially slow twitch or Type I fibres) become damaged because of a relatively high sustained workloads, they have to call upon the far less aerobically-efficient Type II fibres to help maintain a consistent power output an carry the load. This has the effect of reducing what's called your 'exercise economy/efficiency', which essentially means you need to supply more oxygen (and fuel) for the same power output (very similar to the fuel efficiency of a car). This is one of the reasons you can see heart rate drift for a given power as you become fatigued in a race or long training session, since the heart is required to pump more oxygen to sustain the workload once the muscles become fatigued. By improving your exercise economy via greater resistance to fatigue/damage, you're able to improve your endurance at a broad range of intensities.

These low-cadence tempo intervals also specifically target aerobic adaptations within Type IIa muscle fibres, which typically aren't substantially activated in a lower intensity endurance ride until the latter stages of the ride. These intervals are good for reducing VLamax, and thus improving lactate threshold.

2026-05-04.title: Anaerobic Power: 60S Repeats (Level 3)
2026-05-04.description:
30M warm up, building up from 55-65% FTP towards 85-95% FTP, and then settling in to 55-75% FTP. Add in 2-3 10S surges at ~8/10 effort level to fully activate your muscles before the anaerobic efforts.

Then perform 5-6x 1M intervals @ a near maximal effort, paced consistently across the 1M interval (for most people this will be about 130-150% FTP, but for others it may be higher/lower). The first time you do this workout you may need to do this based on feel, but in subsequent workouts you will have a better idea of what power you can target. The goal is to hit a power that you can hold consistently across each of the intervals, so don't go so hard on the first one that you have nothing left for the last ones.

Take 6M for active recovery @ 40-50% FTP (i.e. very easy spinning) after each interval. Take longer to recover if you feel you need to - the focus of this workout is to achieve maximal power in each interval, so the recovery can be as long as necessary to achieve this.

Remaining time up to 1H30M @ 50-65% FTP to return body to resting levels steadily.

See workout details below for further details.

PURPOSE: The main effect of these intervals is to increase peak anaerobic power – that is the maximal rate at which energy can be produced anaerobically. This includes factors such as increased activity of enzymes associated with both the phosphocreatine system and the glycolytic system, and also increased glycogen storage within the muscles (Bangsbo et al., 2006).

In addition, these intervals can also contribute to improved anaerobic stamina through improved buffering of hydrogen ions (Bangsbo et al., 2006), and potential improvements in lactate transport (Pilegaard et al., 1999), aerobic capacity (Burgomaster et al., 2006) and mitochondrial function (Bishop et al., 2014, Tripp et al., 2022).

Bangsbo, J., Mohr, M., Poulsen, A., Perez-Gomez, J., & Krustrup, P. (2006). Training and testing the elite athlete. J Exerc Sci Fit, 4(1), 1-14.

Bishop, D. J., Granata, C., & Eynon, N. (2014). Can we optimise the exercise training prescription to maximise improvements in mitochondria function and content?. Biochimica et Biophysica Acta (BBA)-General Subjects, 1840(4), 1266-1275.

Burgomaster, K. A., Heigenhauser, G. J., & Gibala, M. J. (2006). Effect of short-term sprint interval training on human skeletal muscle carbohydrate metabolism during exercise and time-trial performance. Journal of applied physiology, 100(6), 2041-2047.

Pilegaard, H., Domino, K., Noland, T., Juel, C., Hellsten, Y., Halestrap, A. P., & Bangsbo, J. (1999). Effect of high-intensity exercise training on lactate/H+ transport capacity in human skeletal muscle. American Journal of Physiology-Endocrinology And Metabolism, 276(2), E255-E261.

Tripp, T. R., Frankish, B. P., Lun, V., Wiley, J. P., Shearer, J., Murphy, R. M., & MacInnis, M. J. (2022). Time course and fibre type‐dependent nature of calcium‐handling protein responses to sprint interval exercise in human skeletal muscle. The Journal of Physiology.

2026-04-27.title: Welcome to DBT Coaching
2026-04-27.description:
Let's do this.

Your program is unique to your circumstances and tailored around your fitness levels and goals. From time to time there will be errors, I am human, and also build sessions weekly that might be a little off. But after a while this all will be dialed in.

What makes my coaching different to others is that it's personal. I have not other objection than to help you improve and become a better version of yourself.

Sessions are loaded accordingly, and will be based on time, heart rate, pace and for most on the bike power as well.

These sessions might seem boring at times, but good adaptations may take 6-8 weeks once a stimulus is applied. SO be patient and remember the biggest KEY is consistency.

Apart from constancy is good sleep, solid foundation of whole foods and quality water (hydration).

There are some tough sessions here and it will not be possible to turn up and be 100% every day. Don't stress about that, your aim is to get the frequency in place and then we nail the volume, and then the desired intensity.

If any drastic changes occur in your life, please always share this with your coach.

Most sessions (excluding swim) includes structured workouts that transfers to your training devices such as watches, power meters and smart trainers. Always remember to calibrate smart trainers if you move them around. Power and heart rate batteries should be replaced every 3-4 months as well.

Thank you for signing up and lets make every workout count.

2026-04-27.title: The first few weeks
2026-04-27.description:
The first few weeks of this program might feel easy, it is deliberately so.
Consistency is absolute key to this program, so use the first weeks to get into a sustainable routine.

On days when you do not feel up for the training, I suggest doing the warm up before deciding whether you should continue or not.
If you feel better, continue with the workout.
If you don’t feel better nor worse, continue at an easy pace.
If you do feel worse, take the rest of the day off and rest, your body might need it more than you’re allowing it.

I repeat. Consistency is KEY. Take the rest day as given, and ensure full rest. Overloading will not assist in being consistent. If you are especially tired, please feel free to take the day off or make it an easy day. Rather one day off than a week or two from injury or illness.

Do NOT attempt to catch up on missed workouts. Cramming might lead to doing too much on a given day, thereby affecting the recovery on that day and the training in the following days.

Should you as athlete choose to do additional hours, recovery workouts biking or swimming is advised as this is low impact.
